89 materials

Materials Library

The palette of exterior architecture — and which styles each material defines, with the reasoning behind it.

Adobe / earth

Earth walls with thermal mass drawn straight from the site.

Vernacular

Aluminium panel

Lightweight, precise, factory-finished cladding.

High-Tech

Art glass

Admits light and dissolves the wall between inside and out.

Prairie Style

Ashlar stone

Precisely cut blocks expressing refinement.

Neo-Classical

Black shutters

Operable shutters that shade openings and order the façade.

Colonial Revival

Board-formed concrete

Timber shuttering imprints a warm grain into the concrete.

Brutalism

Breeze-block (concrete)

Structural and sculptural — shapes bold exterior form.

Desert Modernism

Brick

The warm, robust skin of the working building.

IndustrialBrutalism

Brick & stone

Anchoring walls extending outdoors.

Mid-Century Modern

Bronze

Doors, statuary and enrichment.

Neo-ClassicalClassicalBeaux-Arts

Bronze & brass

Rich metalwork for portals and grilles.

Art Deco

Brownstone

Solid, durable and rooted in place — a defining exterior material of the style.

Romanesque Revival

Béton brut (raw concrete)

Cast in place, left exposed, carrying its formwork texture.

Brutalism

Carved stone

Solid, durable and rooted in place — a defining exterior material of the style.

Art Nouveau

Cast iron

Sculptural metalwork for balconies, grilles and ornament.

Victorian

Ceramic tile (trencadís)

Colourful glazed surface for decorative façade pattern.

Art Nouveau

Charred/painted wood

Black or deep-toned timber for contrast and protection.

Scandinavian

Clay tile

Terracotta roofs of the region.

Mediterranean Contemporary

Coloured ducts

Externalised services, colour-coded, celebrated as ornament.

High-Tech

Composite cladding

Lightweight, mouldable panels following curvature.

Parametric

Concrete

Structure and sculptural solidity.

ContemporaryTropical ModernOrganic ArchitectureIndustrial

Copper / bronze

Rich metal for doors, trim and ornamental enrichment.

Organic Architecture

Curved glass

Fluid glazing integrated into the continuous surface.

Parametric

Desert stone

Solid, durable and rooted in place — a defining exterior material of the style.

Desert Modernism

Exposed aggregate

Rugged texture that weathers and reveals substance.

Brutalism

Fair-faced concrete

A single continuous silky surface with precise tie-holes.

Minimalism

Frameless glass

Dissolves boundaries with minimal interruption.

Minimalism

GFRC (glass-fibre concrete)

Cast into smooth double-curved panels.

Parametric

Gilded bronze

Rich metal for doors, trim and ornamental enrichment.

Baroque

Gilded detail

A characteristic material of the style's exterior expression.

Beaux-Arts

Glass

Transparency, lightness and daylight.

ContemporaryScandinavianJapanese MinimalismDesert ModernismOrganic ArchitectureBiophilicDeconstructivismMid-Century ModernSustainable / Passive

Glass curtain wall

Admits light and dissolves the wall between inside and out.

High-Tech

Glazed terracotta

Colourful, repeatable cast ornament.

Art Deco

Granite

Hard, durable stone for robust bases and trim.

Romanesque Revival

Greenery / planting

Living façades and shade integral to the design.

Tropical ModernBiophilicSustainable / Passive

Lead & iron

Sculptural metalwork for balconies, grilles and ornament.

Gothic Revival

Light concrete

Quiet, durable base material.

Scandinavian

Lime render

Soft matte monolithic surface for light to play across.

Minimalism

Limestone

Solid, durable and rooted in place — a defining exterior material of the style.

ClassicalBeaux-Arts

Limestone & marble

Noble, durable, carvable — the medium of the orders.

Neo-Classical

Limestone bands

Solid, durable and rooted in place — a defining exterior material of the style.

Prairie Style

Local stone

Solid, durable and rooted in place — a defining exterior material of the style.

Organic ArchitectureVernacular

Marble

Noble, fine-grained and carvable — conveys permanence and refinement.

ClassicalBeaux-Arts

Marble inlay

Noble, fine-grained and carvable — conveys permanence and refinement.

Renaissance

Metal panel

Crisp, precise, contemporary cladding.

Contemporary

Natural stone

Solid, timeless, in large unbroken planes.

MinimalismJapanese MinimalismMediterranean ContemporaryTropical ModernBiophilic

Painted timber trim

Warm, renewable and tactile — softens and clads the exterior.

Victorian

Perforated metal

Parametric perforation that gradates across the façade.

Parametric

Pietra serena / stone

Solid, durable and rooted in place — a defining exterior material of the style.

Renaissance

Plate glass

Dissolves the inside/outside boundary; admits light.

Modern

Polished stone & marble

Conveys luxury at the base.

Art Deco

Polychrome brick

Robust, modular and warm — the everyday material of solid walls.

VictorianGothic Revival

Recycled materials

Lower embodied carbon and honest texture.

Sustainable / Passive

Red brick

Robust, modular and warm — the everyday material of solid walls.

Colonial Revival

Red sandstone

Solid, durable and rooted in place — a defining exterior material of the style.

Romanesque Revival

Reinforced concrete

Enables the free plan, cantilevers and pilotis.

Modern

Roman brick

Robust, modular and warm — the everyday material of solid walls.

Prairie Style

Rusticated stone

Heavy grooved masonry grounding the base.

Neo-ClassicalRenaissanceRomanesque Revival

Slate (roofs)

Durable dark roofing that crowns the silhouette.

BaroqueBeaux-Arts

Slate roof

Durable dark roofing that crowns the silhouette.

VictorianGothic RevivalRomanesque RevivalColonial Revival

Smooth concrete

Calm monolithic planes in the Ando manner.

Japanese Minimalism

Stained glass

Admits light and dissolves the wall between inside and out.

Art NouveauGothic Revival

Stainless steel / chrome

Gleaming machine-age crowns and trim.

Art Deco

Steel

Slim structure in Case Study houses.

Mid-Century Modern

Steel & aluminium

Slender, strong structure that frees and frames the façade.

Desert Modernism

Stone

Warmth, weight and contextual grounding.

ContemporaryGothic Revival

Stone & marble

Solid, durable and rooted in place — a defining exterior material of the style.

Baroque

Structural steel

Slender frames carry load so walls dissolve into glass.

ModernIndustrialHigh-TechDeconstructivism

Stucco

Smooth render that unifies the wall and catches light.

Prairie StyleRenaissanceBaroqueArt Nouveau

Stucco / lime render

Reflective white/sand walls that stay cool and timeless.

Mediterranean Contemporary

Tension cables

Tension cables that express the flow of structural forces.

High-Tech

Terracotta

Warm fired clay for tiles, ornament and roofs.

ClassicalVictorian

Terracotta tile

Warm fired clay for tiles, ornament and roofs.

Renaissance

Thatch / reed

Local reed roofing, renewable and climate-suited.

Vernacular

Timber (slats & screens)

Lattices and screens filter light and express craft.

Japanese MinimalismMediterranean ContemporaryTropical ModernOrganic ArchitectureBiophilicVernacularSustainable / Passive

Timber cladding

Warm, renewable, local — the signature Nordic skin.

Scandinavian

Timber trim

Warm, renewable and tactile — softens and clads the exterior.

Prairie Style

Titanium / metal cladding

A shimmering skin flowing over complex curves.

Deconstructivism

Travertine

Warm, textured stone for monumental walls and bases.

ClassicalBaroque

Warm timber

Warm, renewable and tactile — softens and clads the exterior.

Desert Modernism

Water

Reflective water that cools and animates the exterior.

Biophilic

Weathered metal

Corrugated and patinated cladding for honest character.

Industrial

White clapboard

Traditional painted timber siding for crisp domestic façades.

Colonial Revival

White render

A pure abstract skin emphasising volume and shadow.

Modern

White trim

Painted classical trim that frames and refines the façade.

Colonial Revival

Whitewash / lime

Breathable traditional plaster with a soft, textured finish.

Vernacular

Wood (beams & cladding)

Warm post-and-beam structure and cladding.

Mid-Century Modern

Wrought iron

Sculptural metalwork for balconies, grilles and ornament.

Art Nouveau

Zinc / stainless steel

Durable fluid cladding for warped surfaces.

Deconstructivism

A practical, exterior-focused architectural design reference — choose a direction and architectural language for any project. Built for design teams.
